title: Bond energies ‚ÄĒ why reactions release or absorb heat
examTip: Energy in (breaking bonds) ‚ąí Energy out (forming bonds) = őĒH. If the answer is negative, the reaction is exothermic. If positive, it is endothermic. Always show your working in two clear steps.
introTips: Breaking bonds always requires energy ‚ÄĒ think of it as pulling atoms apart against the force holding them together.
Forming bonds always releases energy ‚ÄĒ the atoms settle into a more stable arrangement and energy is given out.
The overall reaction is exothermic if more energy is released forming bonds than is needed to break them.
You will be given a bond energy table in the exam ‚ÄĒ you don't need to memorise values, but you must know how to use them.
őĒH = energy in (breaking) ‚ąí energy out (forming). A negative őĒH = exothermic; positive őĒH = endothermic.
intro: Have you ever wondered why burning gas heats your house, or why cold packs for sports injuries get cold when you snap them? Both involve chemical reactions ‚ÄĒ and in both cases, the answer comes down to bonds. Every reaction breaks old bonds and makes new ones. The key is whether more energy is released making new bonds than is needed to break the old ones. If more energy is released than is absorbed, the reaction is exothermic and heat flows out. If more energy is absorbed than released, the reaction is endothermic and heat flows in. Bond energies let you calculate exactly which way ‚ÄĒ and by how much.

keyPoints.heading: Breaking and forming bonds ‚ÄĒ the energy basics
keyPoints.content:
Breaking bonds requires energy ‚ÄĒ you must put energy in to overcome the forces holding atoms together. This is always endothermic.
Forming bonds releases energy ‚ÄĒ as atoms come together into a more stable arrangement, energy is given out. This is always exothermic.

The overall reaction energy depends on which effect dominates:
‚ÄĘ If energy released (forming) > energy required (breaking) ‚Üí exothermic overall
‚ÄĘ If energy required (breaking) > energy released (forming) ‚Üí endothermic overall

Bond energy values are given in kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ and represent the energy needed to break one mole of that type of bond in the gas phase.
writeBullets: Breaking bonds ‚ÄĒ always requires energy (endothermic step)
Forming bonds ‚ÄĒ always releases energy (exothermic step)
Exothermic overall: energy released forming bonds > energy to break bonds
Endothermic overall: energy to break bonds > energy released forming bonds
Bond energies given in kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ

keyTerms.def: The energy needed to break one mole of a particular type of bond in the gas phase, in kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ
A reaction where energy is released to the surroundings; the products have less energy than the reactants
A reaction where energy is absorbed from the surroundings; the products have more energy than the reactants

keyPoints.memTip: BREAK = take in energy (think: it takes effort to break something). MAKE = give out energy (making something stable releases energy).
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often get confused and think breaking bonds releases energy ‚ÄĒ it does not. Emphasise: breaking always costs energy; making always pays energy back.

keyPoints.heading: Calculating őĒH from bond energies
keyPoints.content:
The formula is: őĒH = ő£ energy in (bonds broken) ‚ąí ő£ energy out (bonds formed)

Step-by-step method:
1. Draw out the full structural formula of reactants and products.
2. List every bond broken (reactants) and every bond formed (products).
3. Multiply the bond energy for each bond type by how many of that bond are broken or formed.
4. Calculate: őĒH = total energy in ‚ąí total energy out.

Worked example ‚ÄĒ combustion of hydrogen:
H‚āā + ¬ĹO‚āā ‚Üí H‚āāO
Bonds broken: 1 √ó H‚ąíH (436) + ¬Ĺ √ó O=O (498) = 436 + 249 = 685 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ in
Bonds formed: 2 √ó O‚ąíH (463) = 926 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ out
őĒH = 685 ‚ąí 926 = ‚ąí241 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ (exothermic)
writeBullets: őĒH = ő£ energy in (bonds broken) ‚ąí ő£ energy out (bonds formed)
Step 1: draw structural formulas and list every bond
Step 2: multiply bond energy √ó number of each bond type
Step 3: total energy in ‚ąí total energy out = őĒH
Negative őĒH = exothermic; positive őĒH = endothermic
H‚āā + ¬ĹO‚āā ‚Üí H‚āāO example: őĒH = 685 ‚ąí 926 = ‚ąí241 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ (exothermic)

keyTerms.def: The overall energy change of a reaction; negative = exothermic, positive = endothermic
Sum of ‚ÄĒ meaning you add up all the energy values for bonds broken or formed

keyPoints.memTip: Energy IN (breaking) first, energy OUT (forming) second. IN minus OUT = őĒH. If OUT is bigger than IN, őĒH is negative = exothermic.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The most common error is reversing the formula (putting formed first). Drill the order: broken ‚ąí formed. Also remind students to count every bond, including double bonds counted twice.

keyPoints.heading: Using bond energy tables and common bond values
keyPoints.content:
In the exam you will always be given a bond energy data table. You need to be able to read it and apply it correctly.

Common bond energies (approximate, for practice):
‚ÄĘ C‚ąíH: 412 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ
‚ÄĘ C‚ąíC: 348 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ
‚ÄĘ C=C: 612 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ
‚ÄĘ O=O: 498 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ
‚ÄĘ O‚ąíH: 463 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ
‚ÄĘ C=O: 743 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ (in CO‚āā)
‚ÄĘ H‚ąíH: 436 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ

When a molecule has a double bond (e.g. C=C in alkenes, O=O in oxygen), count it as ONE bond with the double bond energy value ‚ÄĒ do not split it into two single bonds.
writeBullets: Bond energy tables are always provided ‚ÄĒ read them carefully
C‚ąíH: 412, C‚ąíC: 348, C=C: 612, O=O: 498, O‚ąíH: 463 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ (approx.)
Double bonds have their own bond energy ‚ÄĒ do not split them into two singles
Count every bond in the structural formula, including all C‚ąíH bonds

keyTerms.def: A data table giving the energy (kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ) needed to break one mole of each type of bond; provided in the exam

keyPoints.memTip: Draw the structural formula first. Then go through it bond by bond, listing each one. It is easy to miss C‚ąíH bonds ‚ÄĒ count them all.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students frequently miss some C‚ąíH bonds when working with organic molecules. Encourage them to draw full displayed formulas and physically tick each bond as they count it.

keyPoints.heading: Limitations of bond energy calculations
keyPoints.content:
Bond energy calculations give an approximate value for őĒH, not an exact one. This is because:
‚ÄĘ Bond energies are average values ‚ÄĒ the exact energy of a C‚ąíH bond varies slightly depending on which molecule it is in.
‚ÄĘ The calculation assumes all species are in the gas phase, which is not always true in real reactions.

Despite these limitations, bond energy calculations are very useful for predicting whether a reaction is exothermic or endothermic, and for comparing different fuels or reactions.
writeBullets: Bond energy values are averages ‚ÄĒ exact value varies by molecule
Calculations assume gas phase ‚ÄĒ may not match real conditions
Results are approximate but useful for comparing reactions

keyTerms.def: The mean energy needed to break that bond type across a range of different molecules; used because the exact value varies

keyPoints.memTip: Bond energies = averages. Like using average shoe size to fit everyone ‚ÄĒ useful but not perfect.
keyPoints.tutor_note: This point rarely appears as a major question but can appear in 'evaluate' style questions. Worth mentioning briefly.

tables.title: Common bond energies for GCSE calculations
rows: In CO‚āā ‚ÄĒ count twice (two C=O per CO‚āā)
In nitrogen gas (N‚āā) ‚ÄĒ triple bond, very strong

0.question: Which of the following statements about bond breaking is correct?
options: Breaking bonds can be either exothermic or endothermic
Breaking bonds has no energy change
0.correctFeedback: Correct! Breaking bonds always requires energy ‚ÄĒ it is always the endothermic step in any reaction.
0.wrongFeedback: Breaking bonds ALWAYS requires energy ‚ÄĒ it is always endothermic. Forming bonds is what releases energy.

1.question: Using bond energies: H‚āā + Cl‚āā ‚Üí 2HCl. H‚ąíH = 436, Cl‚ąíCl = 242, H‚ąíCl = 431 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. What is őĒH?
1.correctFeedback: Correct! Energy in = 436 + 242 = 678. Energy out = 2 √ó 431 = 862. őĒH = 678 ‚ąí 862 = ‚ąí184 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ (exothermic).
1.wrongFeedback: Energy in (broken): H‚ąíH + Cl‚ąíCl = 436 + 242 = 678. Energy out (formed): 2 √ó H‚ąíCl = 2 √ó 431 = 862. őĒH = 678 ‚ąí 862 = ‚ąí184 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ.

3.question: A reaction has őĒH = +150 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. What does this tell you?
options: The reaction is endothermic ‚ÄĒ more energy was absorbed breaking bonds than released forming them
The reaction is exothermic ‚ÄĒ energy was released to the surroundings
More energy was released forming bonds than absorbed breaking them
The reaction has no energy change
3.correctFeedback: Spot on! A positive őĒH means endothermic ‚ÄĒ more energy was needed to break bonds than was released when new bonds formed.
3.wrongFeedback: Positive őĒH = endothermic. The energy absorbed breaking bonds was greater than the energy released forming bonds. If őĒH were negative, the reaction would be exothermic.

commonMistakes: Reversing the formula: calculating energy out ‚ąí energy in instead of energy in ‚ąí energy out. Always do bonds broken minus bonds formed.
Forgetting to count all C‚ąíH bonds in organic molecules ‚ÄĒ draw the full displayed formula and tick each bond as you go.
Treating a double bond as two separate single bonds ‚ÄĒ it has its own bond energy value (e.g. C=C = 612, not 2 √ó 348).
Concluding a reaction is exothermic when őĒH is positive ‚ÄĒ positive őĒH means endothermic, negative means exothermic.
Not multiplying bond energies by the number of moles in the equation ‚ÄĒ if there are 4 C‚ąíH bonds, you must count all four.
examTips: Always draw the full structural (displayed) formula for both reactants and products before you start counting bonds ‚ÄĒ it prevents you missing bonds.
Write out two clear subtotals: 'Energy in (bonds broken) = ...' and 'Energy out (bonds formed) = ...' before calculating őĒH ‚ÄĒ examiners want to see your working.
If őĒH comes out negative, state 'exothermic'. If positive, state 'endothermic'. Always include this conclusion for the final mark.
Bond energy values are averages ‚ÄĒ if asked to evaluate or describe limitations, say that values are mean values and the actual energy varies with the molecule.
revisionCardBullets: Breaking bonds: always requires energy (endothermic step)
Forming bonds: always releases energy (exothermic step)
Exothermic overall: energy released forming > energy to break
Endothermic overall: energy to break > energy released forming
őĒH = ő£ energy in (bonds broken) ‚ąí ő£ energy out (bonds formed)
Negative őĒH = exothermic; positive őĒH = endothermic
Double bonds have their own energy value ‚ÄĒ do not split them
Count every bond in the displayed formula before calculating
Bond energies are average values ‚ÄĒ calculations give approximate őĒH
Show two subtotals in workings: total in and total out
